[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 3: Abuse of Dominance and Competition Law | Section: a0016 - INDICES OF DOMINANCE: ‘ALL OR ANY FACTOR’]

The CCI on the basis of the investigation report of the DG noted that though Android OS theoretically is an open-source project, it was actually controlled by Google. It found that in order to have access to the proprietary apps of Google, including Play Store, Play Services, Google Search, etc., as well as the trademarks related to Android, OEMs have to sign multiple agreements with Google such as Mobile Application Distribution Agreement (MADA), Anti-fragmentation Agreement (AFA), Android Compatibility Commitment Agreement (ACC) and Revenue Sharing Agreement (RSA). The cumulative effect of these agreements has been described in the following flowchart:

Hence, due to the compatibility obligations of OEMs following these agreements, they were restrained from manufacturing and selling devices incorporating the forked version of Android OS. Therefore, any OEM who wants to offer these ‘must have’ services to its customers must sign and accept the restrictions laid down in MADA, AFA/ACC and, thereafter, RSAs to maximize the revenue. Hence, competing general search service providers were not in a position to nullify the competitive edge that Google secured for itself through pre-installation as well as premium placement under MADA. This meant that Google had manufactured unyielding consumer dependence in its favour. The anti-competitive effects of these agreements, as has been elaborated by the CCI, have been discussed under abuse.

Herein, it is pointed out that in appeal, Google argued before NCLAT that all three agreements (MADA, AFA/ACC, RSA) should be separately looked into. However, the NCLAT rejected this on the premise that when all three agreements are signed by an OEM, its consequence has to be conjointly looked into. This implies that when the subject of all three agreements relate to Android open source and are interrelated, they have to be read conjointly to examine their cumulative effect to examine the dependence of consumers and the extent of network effects as a barrier to entry.

The CCI in its decisional practice of abuse of dominance, has always looked into entry barriers while determining whether an entity is dominant or not. It has been noted that low barriers to entry or expansion by actual or potential competitors can deter a company from raising prices if expansion or entry would be likely, timely and sufficient. Having said that, expansion or entry must be immediate and of sufficient scope and magnitude to constitute an efficient, competitive constraint on the enterprise concerned.

Barriers to entry can include not only legal barriers but also advantages peculiar to the dominant company. They include all those behaviour patterns which a new entrant will have to follow in order to be sustainable in the market. Such patterns may be a result of the dominant incumbent. The CCI also considers the behaviour of the dominant undertaking, which may in turn become a market barrier for other enterprises in the market. For example, in the NSE case, NSE started providing transaction waivers only in the currency derivative market. In such a situation where the rules of the game are set by the dominant incumbent, a new player has no option but to match the behaviour of the dominant enterprise, in this case, which was providing transaction waivers to start with to enter the said market, leave alone being competitive.

[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 2: Anti-competitive Agreements and Competition Law | Section: a0056 - TYPES OF VERTICAL AGREEMENTS]

Similarly, in Arshiya Rail Infrastructure Ltd. (ARIL) v. Ministry of Railway (MoR) & Others, the CCI held that tie-in arrangement is used by an entity which has a dominant position in one product/service (i.e., transportation services – tying service) and ties it with a laggard product (i.e., maintenance of container – tied service) to promote the laggard product. However, there was no tie-in arrangement in the said case because none of the entities were in the business of maintenance services. As per the facts, MoR granted a licence and concessionaire agreement entered with fifteen private operators to move container trains on the railway lines on a licence basis. The informants, who were private operators, alleged that MoR, Indian Railway and Container Corporation of India, had indulged in abusive conduct as a group or a single economic entity in the market for the transport of freight via railways. One of the allegations was on violation of section 3(4)(a) of the Competition Act because MoR insisted that maintenance of container trains can only be sourced from it. However, the CCI dismissed this allegation by saying that the supplementary condition to obtain a fitness certificate of train from MoR does not amount to a tie-in arrangement in terms of section 3(4)(a) of the Competition Act. The CCI observed that MoR imposed such conditions to maintain railway operation and protect the overall safety of passengers.

Explanation (b) to section 3(4) of the Competition Act defines an exclusive dealing agreement as an exclusive agreement imposed by the seller which restricts the buyer from purchasing any goods or services other than those of the seller. Exclusive dealing agreement also covers the vertical restraint imposed by the buyer on the seller from dealing with other buyers. The seller who is accepting this restrictive term has no option but to supply its product or service to the designated purchaser as per the condition. For example, Manufacturer A entered into an exclusive dealing agreement with all the retailers to buy only the product of Manufacturer A or the authorized dealers of Manufacturer A.

Prior to the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, this explanation was called an ‘exclusive supply agreement’, and it used to cover only the vertical agreement where restraint was exclusively imposed on the buyer by the seller. So, the Competition Act was amended to recognize the imposition of the exclusive agreement from both the seller side and the buyer side.

The issue of exclusive dealing agreement (supply-side) was discussed in the Shri Shamsher Kataria v. Honda Siel Cars India Ltd. and Others (‘Automobile spare parts case’), where both the CCI and the erstwhile COMPAT held that the authorized dealers were prohibited from sourcing spare parts from any other enterprise, apart from the OEMs. These exclusive agreements imposed restrictions on the buyer side of the market to buy and sell the spare parts exclusively; hence, section 3(4)(b) of the Competition Act was violated. In another case, the CCI in M/s ESYS Information Technologies Pvt. Ltd. v. Intel Corporation and Others held that exclusive agreement means that the agreement by the seller restricts the distributors from dealing with any competitors of the seller. In the absence of any evidence, the CCI was unable to show that the concerned agreement with Intel exclusively imposed a restriction on the distributors to deal with Intel. Also, there was evidence which showed that Intel allowed the distributors to deal with other sellers.

[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 3: Abuse of Dominance and Competition Law | Section: a0032 - TYPES OF ABUSE]

Unfair terms are often in the nature of mandatory acceptance of the dominant firm’s policies, even if they are harmful. CCI’s decisional practice indicates that contractual terms that are one-sided with no option besides acceptance are unfair. For instance, in the WhatsApp case, WhatsApp updated its Terms of Service and Privacy Policy for users to the effect that consent to sharing and integration of user data with other Facebook Companies for a range of purposes, including marketing and advertising, was made a precondition for availing WhatsApp service. There was also opacity, vagueness and open-endedness about the nature, kind and extent of data sharing that the user was being compelled to consent to. There was also no option to opt out of the said Policy update, and the users now have to mandatorily agree if they want to continue using WhatsApp Messenger. On this basis, the CCI ordered an investigation into WhatsApp’s conduct for imposing unfair terms by propagating a take-it-or-leave-it’ policy. Subsequently, WhatsApp challenged the jurisdiction of CCI to pass such an order before the Delhi High Court owing to potentially conflicting opinions when the Supreme Court was already looking into whether the Policy was violative of the right to privacy under Article 21 of the Constitution. However, the Delhi High Court clarified that the investigation by the CCI is confined to whether the Policy furthers the dominant position occupied by WhatsApp and institutes anti-competitive practices. The Supreme Court is not analysing the Policy through the prism of competition law. The spheres of operation of both are vastly different. Hence, it allowed the investigation to proceed. The Delhi High also affirmed the prima facie findings of CCI that WhatsApp’s dominance due to lack of/restricted interoperability between platforms, its ability to engage in data concentration and the opacity, vagueness, open-endedness and incomplete disclosures in the Policy have the potential to undermine the competitive processes and create further barriers to market entry.

A key concept of unfair conduct is exclusionary conduct, was seen in the Google Android case. The complex, self-reinforcing and pervasive nature of Google’s agreements have been described earlier. The collective unfair impositions under these agreements, as elaborated by the CCI, have been described in the following diagram:

By virtue of the agreements discussed above, Google ensured that users continued to use its search services on mobile devices which facilitated uninterrupted growth of advertisement revenue for Google. Further, it also helped Google to further invest and improve its services to the exclusion of others. Thus, the underlying objective of Google in imposing various restrictions via MADA, AFA/ACC and RSAs was to protect and strengthen its dominant position in general search services and, thus, its revenues via search advertisements.

[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 6: Digital Markets and Competition Law | Section: a0031 - TRENDS FROM DECISIONAL PRACTICE]

In appeal, the NCLAT overturned CCI’s direction on the ground that the APIs which provide essential services to the apps which are part of the Play Store, are privately developed, maintained and continually updated by Google and are provided by Google to app developers and OEMs. Consequently, unhindered access to such proprietary items of Google cannot be given to app developers, OEMs and Google’s existing and potential competitors. Further, such proprietary software which is ‘developed through scientific and technical innovation should fetch value to Google and, therefore, remain an incentive for a technological company/Google to further carry out such development and monetize it through its commercial use’. Thus, unhindered access to the same cannot be allowed without going through necessary technical and commercial engagement with Google. The matter is pending appeal before the Supreme Court.

This was also recognized by the CCI in the AIOVA v. Flipkart, wherein it observed that since the technology-driven and marketplace-based e-commerce model is still evolving and relatively nascent, regard must be had to the growth potential as well as the efficiencies and consumer benefits that such markets can provide. Hence, any intervention in such markets needs to be carefully crafted lest it stifles innovation.

CCI has refrained from acting as a price regulator. In the Play Store case, CCI did not hold that the 15%-30% commission charged by Google through imposition of a mandatory GPBS on each transaction pertaining to a paid app/digital in-app purchase was excessive. Similarly, in the MMT-Go case, CCI did not give a finding on deep discounting by MMT-Go. Thus, it is clear that CCI has refrained itself from acting as a price regulator or regulating the commercial wisdom of enterprises. Rather, the CCI has focused on freeing the market at every level of the value chain by removing bottlenecks and promoting dynamic efficiency and innovation in the market (such as by issuing remedial directions). Therefore, the stand taken by the CCI is pro-competition without interfering with the business decisions of the platforms.

[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 4: Mergers and Competition Law | Section: a0056 - PROCEDURE FOR INVESTIGATION OF A COMBINATION]

In its decisional practice, to date, the CCI has not refused approval to any combination, even in cases of belated filing or gun-jumping, provided the notice was subsequently filed. When a combination is likely to cause AAEC in India, the CCI undertakes a detailed enquiry into the combination, including inviting opinions from relevant third parties and allows parties to submit voluntary modifications or propose modifications of their own. This procedure has been discussed below.

The scheme of the Competition Act and the Combination Regulations provides that the CCI will form a prima facie opinion on whether the proposed combination is likely to cause any AAEC in India.

Regulation 19 of the Combination Regulations provides the prima facie opinion will be formed by the CCI within thirty days of the receipt of the merger notification. In the event that the CCI forms a prima facie view that the combination would not cause AAEC on competition in the relevant market in India, CCI approves the combination, under section 31 of the Competition Act, in Phase I itself. The said period of thirty days of forming a prima facie opinion does not include clock stops. It must be noted that even in Phase 1, Regulation 19 empowers the CCI to call for information from any third party to enquire if the combination has caused or is likely to cause any AAEC in India. As such, the CCI has used Regulation 19(3) of the Combination Regulations to call for information from Air India while approving the Jet/Etihad deal.

Regulation 19(2) of the Combination Regulations provides that the CCI may ask the parties to the combination to accept modifications for forming its prima facie order. The CCI has previously sought behavioural commitments in the form of commitments in its combination decisions in Phase I itself, where it asked the parties to review its existing contracts to ensure that they are in compliance with the provisions of the Competition Act and the parties continue to abide by the provisions of the sectoral regulator.

In this regard, it is apposite to note that in July 2015, the CCI amended the filing requirements prescribed under the Combinations Regulations with the aim to make the filing simpler and readily acceptable to various stakeholders, thereby aligning its practice with other matured anti-trust jurisdictions. The key element introduced is the role of third parties in the merger approval process: third parties can give their comments on the likely competition concerns arising in the relevant market as a result of the combination. This has been discussed in more detail subsequently.

[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 6: Digital Markets and Competition Law | Section: a0006 - CHARACTERISTIC FEATURES OF DIGITAL MARKETS]

The primary source of revenue for digital platforms collecting data is through monetization of this data in the form of advertisements. Further, with large data to process, an enterprise has the opportunity to anticipate consumer needs and demand in more than one sector. Armed with this data, the enterprise can create its own products/services which the data shows consumers may prefer. This can be seen in emerging digital markets, as discussed in the later parts of this chapter.

The Big Tech Report also analysed how access to data gives an advantage to digital platforms by enabling them to ‘collect and store large amounts of data for comprehensively tracking and profiling end customers and business users. Privileged data from one market may be, thus, used by a leading platform in another market to gain competitive advantage. This further leads to the position of the leading platform getting entrenched.’

In the Google Android case, it was found that mandatory pre-installation of Google search (widget on the default home screen) on all Android devices under MADA ensures user traffic and valuable user data for Google that it can collect, combine and leverage for its search advertising business. Hence, through MADA requirements, Google was in a position to leverage its dominant position in the Android ecosystem to maintain and strengthen its dominance in the search advertising sector. The data generated from each app of Google, such as Google Search, Maps, YouTube, etc., can complement the other and, thus, gives ‘Google a big data advantage over its rivals. Importantly, it also raises the willingness to pay by advertisers. Thus, in presence of cross market data advantage, pushing Android with other apps is a business strategy for Google.’

In the Play Store case also, it was found that Google uses the data of the app developers to support its vast advertisement business by, among other things, selling access to the data back to the developers. This observation was strengthened by virtue of the advertising revenue earned by Google through advertisements in the Google Play Store.

Therefore, an analysis of the CCI and NCLAT’s findings on data reveals that data is a factor of market control, and access to large troves of data allows a firm to further entrench its market power. Access to data has wide-ranging implications on competition right from securing a competitive advantage, using it to create entry barriers and foreclosing competition by improving one’s own innovation to the exclusion of others and also expanding into new markets. Since firms cannot be stopped altogether from exploiting the data that they have gathered, at best, they need to be cautious about not abusing such access as a tool to prevent their rivals from competing on merits.

Across Platform Parity Agreements (APPAs), also called Retail Most Favoured Nation (MFN) clauses, are stipulations where the sellers guarantee an online platform, terms (whether price and/or non-price) that are at least as favourable as those granted to any other platform, thus ensuring the former an advantage over its competitors. By securing such favourable terms, the platforms attempt to guarantee the best available price and/or terms for a given product to its final consumers.

The Competition Law Review Committee also noted in its Report that: MFN clauses can potentially give rise to both pro-competitive benefits and anti-competitive effects on the market. The extent of the effect depends on various factors such as the market structure, the form of the MFN clause used, and the characteristics of the sellers and buyers in the market. In light of this Committee agreed that such agreements should be analysed under the ‘rule of reason’ or ‘effects’ test under Section 3(4) of the Competition Act. Having said that, the Committee noted that in cases where such clauses seek to facilitate a cartel, fix price or supply terms, it will be hit by the provisions of Section 3(3) of the Competition Act.

[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 2: Anti-competitive Agreements and Competition Law | Section: a0031 - STRUCTURAL FACTORS AIDING CARTELIZATION]

While cartelization can happen in any sector, the CCI has noted that there are certain industries which are more prone to cartelization than other industries due to certain inherent structural factors prevalent in those industries. For instance, there are numerous investigations in the pharmaceutical industry, film and entertainment industry, tyre industry, cement industry, paper industry, etc. Although the CCI has observed that just because such industry characteristics are present, it does not ipso facto mean that market participants have indulged in a cartel. In fact, there have been instances where the CCI has noted that although the industry has the structural characteristics that aid cartelization, the market participants have not indulged in cartelization (e.g., the Tyre case). However, as a part of economic analysis, the CCI does look at the industry characteristics in all cartel cases.

The CCI has, in its decisions, referred to the following structural factors in an industry which are prone to cartelization.

The CCI has noted that in an oligopolistic market, it is more likely that each market participant is aware of the other participants’ behaviour. In an oligopolistic market, interdependence between firms is an important characteristic which would imply that each firm in such a market considers the likely reactions of other firms while making independent decisions particularly regarding prices and output. Though oligopolistic markets can lead to competitive outcomes, the outcomes may not always be market-driven but rather a result of concerted effort or collusion. Oligopoly markets are infamous for being recognized as the perfect market for cartels to thrive by charging higher prices at a supra-competitive level. The Supreme Court in the LPG-I case and the NCLAT in the LPG-II case discuss the ‘Theory of Oligopolistic Interdependence’ by saying that the oligopoly market itself facilitates cartels because the market players are interdependent, they are aware of each other’s presence and follow each other’s business strategies. Interdependency allows them to collude without any direct or indirect exchange of information, hence, creating a realm of conscious parallelism which lies outside the scope of competition law. In this regard, it is apposite to point out the observations of the CCI in the Tyre case: In a market which is oligopolistic in nature, it is more likely that each market player is aware of the actions of the other and influences each other’s decisions. No doubt, interdependence between firms is an important characteristic of such a market which would mean that each firm in such a market takes into account the likely reactions of other firms while making independent decisions particularly as regards prices and output. Though oligopolistic markets can lead to competitive outcomes, the outcomes may not always be market driven but rather the result of concerted effort or collusion. The interdependence between firms can lead to collusion – both implicit as well as explicit. Thus, high concentration may provide a structural reasoning for collusive action resulting in parallelism (price or output), yet it is very important to differentiate between ‘rational’ conscious parallelism arising out of the interdependence of the firms’ strategic choices and parallelism stemming from purely concerted action. Thus, inferring of cartels would require further evidences. Economic theory has demonstrated convincingly that ‘conscious parallelism’, is not uncommon in homogeneous oligopolistic markets. Competing firms are bound to be conscious of one another’s activities in all phases, including marketing and pricing. Aware of such outcomes especially where there is little real difference in product the Commission is of the opinion that it is quite probable that in many such instances, conscious parallelism may be dictated solely by economic necessity. Avoidance of price wars is a common instance where this takes place.

[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 1: Introduction to Competition Law | Section: a0001 - MARCH TOWARDS COMPETITION LAW]

Further, the Competition Act seeks to maintain a level playing field between the private and the public sector and seek competitive neutrality; i.e., government or state-owned enterprises should not use their legislative or fiscal powers to advantage their own businesses over the private sector. The provisions of the Competition Act are applicable to any enterprise which carries on economic activity, irrespective of whether it is publicly or privately owned. We will see from the definition of enterprise, discussed in detail below, that all the commercial activities of the government, excluding the sovereign functions (energy, currency, defence and space), come under the provisions of the Competition Act and that no antitrust exemptions are applicable to such state-owned enterprise. In fact, the CCI has penalized state-owned enterprises, like Coal India, since they have indulged in activities which constitute abuse of dominance. The erstwhile Competition Appellate Tribunal (COMPAT) also upheld the order passed by the CCI against Coal India. Thereafter, in Coal India Ltd. v. CCI and Another (‘Coal India case’), Coal India appealed before the Supreme Court by arguing that Coal India is a monopoly created by the Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973 with an objective of protecting it from challenges, and is guarded under Article 31B of the Constitution of India, 1950. Article 31B validates certain enactments (such as, the Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973) which have been inserted under the Ninth Schedule of the Constitution of India, 1950. Coal India also relied upon section 19(4)(g) of the Competition Act to say that Coal India is not a dominant player under the Competition Act because Coal India is dominant due to a statute; hence, it should be treated differently that an ordinary dominant player. It was argued that Coal India was created under the said statute for the equitable distribution of coal in order to secure the ‘common good’ under Article 39(b) of the Constitution of India, 1950. Accordingly, Coal India took support of Article 39(b) of the Constitution of India, 1950, to say that it has not violated the Competition Act because it is following the principle of ‘common good’ while distributing minerals and resources of the highest importance for India’s economy. Hence, the provisions of the Competition Act will not be applicable to the conduct of Coal India because Coal India is an instrument of the state, and its conduct is in accordance with the directive principles under Article 39(b). Furthermore, it was also argued that there is no abuse of dominance under section 4(2)(a) and (b) of the Competition Act because the conduct of Coal India relating to the pricing and production of coals is based on the constitutional mandate under Article 39(b) and the objective of Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973. Additionally, it was also argued that section 28 of the Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973 is a non-obstante clause; hence, it prevails over the Competition Act.

[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 5: Intellectual Property and Competition Law | Section: a0007 - SCOPE OF THE IPR EXEMPTION]

Further, the various legal provisions and the decisional practice of CCI with respect to section 4 are covered in the chapter on abuse of dominance. It has also been confirmed by the CCI, in the Automobile Spare Parts case, that the safe harbour of IPR exemption is not applicable for abuse of dominance actions mentioned under section 4(2) of the Competition Act. Therefore, if an enterprise is found to be dominant pursuant to Explanation (a) to section 4(2) and is indulging in practices amounting to denial of market access or refusal to deal, it is no defence to suggest that such exclusionary conduct is within the scope of their IPR. In appeal, the erstwhile COMPAT reiterated the observations of the CCI that abusive practices under section 4 of the Competition Act cannot be protected under the defence of section 3(5)(i).

However, the stringent approach to non-applicability of the IPR defence to section 4 has been substantially diluted by the NCLAT to the extent that a dominant enterprise cannot be forced to share its proprietary rights in Umar Javeed and ors. v. Google LLC and Anr (‘Google Android case’), which has been discussed in detail in the chapter on abuse of dominance. In this case, the question arose of access to Google’s APIs, i.e., its intellectual property to its rivals, OEMs and app developers. Google owns the IPR to the Android Operating System (OS), and as per its Brand Guidelines, the ‘Android’ name and the Android logo are property of Google LLC and not part of the assets available through the Android Open-Source Project.

Further, the use of the ‘Android’ trademark on hardware, packaging or marketing materials of devices is restricted to Android-compatible devices only. Therefore, to use these IPRs, the OEMs must adhere to the obligations imposed by Google. Accordingly, the CCI observed that Google controls the licensing of trademarks related to Android, and OEMs are dependent on Google for using IPR related to Android. Consequently, CCI directed that: ‘Google shall not deny access to its Play Services APIs to disadvantage OEMs, app developers and its existing or potential competitors. This would ensure interoperability of apps between Android OS which complies with compatibility requirements of Google and Android Forks…’ In an appeal before the NCLAT, Google argued that it cannot be asked to share APIs as it constitutes the IPR of Google and that the CCI did not demonstrate that Play Services APIs fulfil the criteria of ‘essential facility’. Interestingly, the NCLAT affirmed the argument of Google by holding that Google’s APIs constitute its proprietary software into which Google has made investments for scientific and technical development. Thus, Google is allowed to enjoy the fruits of its labour. Accordingly, the NCLAT set aside the direction of CCI to allow open access to Google’s APIs. This is an important observation as it accounts for one of the rare cases where a dominant firm has been allowed to restrict access to its IPR. The other aspects of this case have been discussed in more detail under the heading of compulsory licensing. It is relevant to mention that the said observations are presently under challenge before the Supreme Court.

[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 3: Abuse of Dominance and Competition Law | Section: a0032 - TYPES OF ABUSE]

(e) Supply of ungraded coal: When the price of coal is based on the grade/quality, the buyer has the right to get the grade for which he is paying the price. However, the Fuel Supply Agreement did not impose a strict liability upon Coal India and its subsidiaries to supply only the agreed grades but only mentioned making adequate arrangements to assess the quality and for providing monitoring mechanism to prevent loading of ungraded coal. It merely provided that Coal India need only try that the ungraded coal is not loaded, however, if the ungraded coal was loaded and transported, there was no provision for compensation. Further, even in case of supply of ungraded coal which was in breach of the agreed quality under the Fuel Supply Agreement, the buyer was saddled with all the expenses on transportation, royalty, taxes, etc., without any justification. However, other players in the market reimbursed the freight costs borne by the buyer in case of supply of ungraded coal. The CCI did note that the ungraded coal may have some value and Coal India may be able to sell such ungraded coal in the open market to the willing buyer. However, forcing the buyer who has specifically contracted for supply of graded coal to pay for such goods transported by default was not fair in any circumstances. The CCI held that Coal India cannot absolve itself of the responsibility of supplying only graded coal. In this regard, it is apposite to note the observations of the CCI:the consumers cannot be forced to pay the cost of ungraded coal. Such condition not only gives an upper hand to the seller but also reduces its commitment to supply right quality of coal. The conduct of the seller is guided by the FSA and if there is no obligation on the seller to assure the supply of only graded coal, the coal companies are not bound to do the same. [emphasis supplied] It is not out of place to mention that such provisions also result in misappropriation or mishandling of the limited resources of railways in supply of rake to the coal industry as well as of the buyer. The ultimate sufferer is the end user of power on whom the increased cost is passed on. Thus, the terms and conditions in FSA regarding supply of quality coal should be guided by the strict adherence to the desired quality and the measures relating to grading, sampling and testing of the coal needs to be incorporated in the agreement to the satisfaction of both the parties. (f) Compensation for supply of oversized, wet and sticky coal: In the process of supply of coal, there are chances of dispatch of oversized, wet and sticky coal along with stones. For any Fuel Supply Agreement to have a semblance of fairness, it must necessarily provide for payment of compensation in such an event and operate in a non-discriminatory manner among all classes of buyers. However, the compensation under the Fuel Supply Agreement for new power producers was capped at 0.75% of the total quantity of coal, whereas there was no cap on compensation for existing power producers. Hence, the CCI held that the same is ex facie unfair since the cap was not based on actual quantity or any other reasonable basis besides discrimination between the two classes of buyers. (g) Unilateral termination of agreement: The CCI noted that Coal India enjoys a situation of statutory monopoly, and the buyers are heavily dependent upon Coal India. In light of the same, insertion of a clause reserving the right to unilaterally terminate the agreement without having scope for review by any independent agency is not fair under the extant regulatory framework operating in the coal sector.

[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 6: Digital Markets and Competition Law | Section: a0006 - CHARACTERISTIC FEATURES OF DIGITAL MARKETS]

For instance, in the MMT-Go case, MMT-Go had argued that parity conditions are necessary to prevent free riding. However, the CCI held that the free-riding concerns were mitigated due to the discounts offered by MMT-Go to build its network as well as the increased competition between OTAs, as these factors together meant that users do not have any incentive to free ride. Further, any investments made by MMT-Go on its platform were not hotel-specific, so even if hotels did engage in free riding, that did not take away MMT-Go’s incentives to invest in its platform. Yet, narrow parity clauses were found to be justifiable. The relevant extracts are as follows:MMT-Go claims the parity condition to be driven by the need to address free riding concerns. The Commission is of the view that the potential free riding problem does not have any perceptible relevance in the presence of the discounts that MMT-Go offers over and above the rate at which a hotel lists its rooms. When looked at in light of the deep discounts that MMT-Go has been offering over the years (as the data on record bring out clearly), it does not appear to be a realistic possibility that a user would discover a hotel on MMT-Go and then book it through another channel/OTA at a higher price point. It is MMT-Go’s own submission that discounts were provided to build network. Thus, through its discounting strategy MMT-Go ensured both user footfalls and conversions on its platform. Further, when hotels have more OTA choices, the increased competition between the OTAs may lower fees/commission paid to hotels, so consumers may not have as much incentive to free ride. Moreover, what emerged from the cases dealt by other authorities, it has been observed that there may not be any concern about the effect of free riding on incentives to invest (in case of OTAs) because the investments of the platform are not “specific” to a particular hotel – thus, if there is free riding on the part of a particular hotel, this does not undermine the incentive of the platform to invest overall. Be that as it may, the Commission finds some merit in the finding of the DG that the narrow price parity clauses may be justifiable on the grounds of free riding problem. Hotels and OTA are in a contractual relationship and therefore, free riding of hotels on the investment made by OTAs in advertisement and promotion can be detrimental to the business of OTAs. However, wide price parity obligation is not justifiable as it reduces the competition between the OTAs and may have an adverse impact on prices charged to end-consumers.

Therefore, the discussion on parity clauses can be concluded by stating that the free-riding concerns of a platform in terms of its incentives to invest have to be balanced with the freedom of business users to list on multiple distribution channels without being restricted in terms of price since ultimately competition largely boils down to competing on price. It is exactly this balance (which narrow parity clauses seem to meet) that CCI analyses in checking whether parity obligations are anticompetitive or not.

[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 2: Anti-competitive Agreements and Competition Law | Section: a0006 - INDIRECT EVIDENCE: ROLE OF CIRCUMSTANTIAL EVIDENCE]

Similarly, In Re: Alleged anti-competitive conduct in the Beer Market in India (‘Beer cartel case’), the CCI initiated a suo motu investigation pursuant to the receipt of a leniency application by Crown Beers India Private Limited and SABMillers India Limited under section 46 of the Competition Act read with Regulation 5 of the Competition Commission of India (Lesser Penalty) Regulations, 2009. From the evidence collected from the leniency application, the CCI prima facie noted that in several states and union territories, price fixation and implementation of price adjustment existed in the beer industry. After a holistic analysis of the evidence under the leniency application and the DG report, the CCI concluded the existence of a beer cartel in the ten states and union territories and found that the beer cartel violated section 3(3)(a), (b) and (c) of the Competition Act. The beer manufacturers reduced their beer production to create an artificial scarcity of their product in the market. The beer manufacturers were also sharing their commercial information, such as beer production, revenue details, targets, stock sold and stock held by the companies, and details of their stocks lying with the distributors. This was done to limit the production of beer in the market and put pressure on the government to lower the excise duty on beer. The CCI found both direct evidence and indirect evidence to hold such anti-competitive to be violative of section 3(3)(b) of the Competition Act.

As a part of a cartel investigation, the DG analyses the profit margin of each of the market participants to see whether there is a trend which shows the existence of an agreement. In the Cement cartel case, the CCI noted the cement manufacturing-wise trend in profit margins and net profit. Because of cartelization, cement manufacturers were able to make huge profits over the years by limiting and controlling supplies and by imposing a higher cost per cement bag. Furthermore, the net profit of the cement manufacturers shows that they were able to make a high net profit by eliminating competition and charging unreasonable prices.

[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 1: Introduction to Competition Law | Section: a0045 - JURISDICTIONAL BOUNDARY OF CCI]

Interestingly, the Supreme Court in the Bharati Airtel case further held that TRAI does not have the exclusive jurisdiction to deal with matters related to anti-competitive practices in the telecom sector. The TRAI is an expert in dealing with matters pertaining to telecom services, such as unfair conditions in the agreements of telecom operators and denial of the POIs. Whereas the CCI is an expert regulator of the market and overall economy. The CCI is better equipped to deal with the practices having appreciable adverse effects on the competition, to promote competition in the market, and protect the business users as well as the end customers. So, both regulators operate in different fields, and they can co-exist. But as the TRAI is the most appropriate regulator, first it needs to opine on the jurisdictional facts as to whether IDO were under any obligation to provide the POIs and quality issues during the testing period, then only the CCI can start the inquiry under section 26 of the Competition Act, and thereby decide whether the agreement between IDOs was anti-competitive in nature or not. The Supreme Court concluded by making the following observations:In a nutshell, that leads to the conclusion that the view taken by the High Court is perfectly justified. Even the argument of the learned ASG is that the exercise of jurisdiction by CCI to investigate an alleged cartel does not impinge upon TRAI’s jurisdiction to regulate the industry in any way. It was submitted that the promotion of competition and prevention of competitive behaviour may not be high on the change of sectoral regulator which makes it prone to ‘regulatory capture’ and, therefore, CCI is competent to exercise its jurisdiction from the standpoint of the Competition Act. However, having taken note of the skilful exercise which TRAI is supposed to carry out, such a comment vis-à-vis TRAI may not be appropriate. No doubt, as commented by the Planning Commission in its report of February 2007, a sectoral regulator, may not have an overall view of the economy as a whole, which CCI is able to fathom. Therefore, our analysis does not bar the jurisdiction of CCI altogether but only pushes it to a later stage, after TRAI has undertaken necessary exercise in the first place, which it is more suitable to carry out.

Further, the Bombay High Court in Star India Pvt. Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India set aside the CCI’s prima facie order directing DG to investigate the conduct of Star India Pvt. Ltd. and Sony Pictures Network India Pvt. Ltd. causing refusal to deal with Noida Software Technology Park Ltd. The High Court of Bombay relied on the Bharati Airtel case and held that CCI has no jurisdiction to pass an order to investigate when the matter is pending before the Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellant Tribunal. Similarly, in the Trustees Association of India and Other v. Competition Commission of India and Other, a writ was filed before the Bombay High Court challenging the CCI’s prima facie order. The writ petitioner argued that CCI is holding a separate investigation on the issue which is pending before the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI). The Bombay High Court passed a stay order against the CCI’s inquiry until SEBI concluded its enquiry and formed its prima facie view. The Bombay High Court also directed SEBI to complete its enquiry within sixty days and to form its prima facie opinion on the matter. Further, it was also directed to SEBI that if it concludes that there is no violation of the SEBI Act, 1992, and the matter should properly proceed before CCI, then whatever material SEBI has obtained shall be transmitted to CCI to save time in DG’s investigation. The objective behind such directions is to prevent a scenario of conflicting orders due to parallel investigations by two different regulatory authorities into the same complaint.

[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 1: Introduction to Competition Law | Section: a0063 - RELEVANT MARKET]

However, market definition, particularly in digital markets, is not a static concept, and the relevant market assessment must be done at each point in time based on the prevailing circumstances. Hence, CCI shifted from the narrow approach of painting online and offline channels with the same brush to thoroughly account for the challenges in digital markets and effectively enforce the Competition Act on online market players by delineating online markets as distinct relevant market. For instance, the CCI shifted its approach while delineating relevant market in All India Online Vendors Association v. Flipkart India Pvt. Ltd. In this case, it was alleged that Flipkart was engaged in deep discounting, denied market access and leveraged its position to enter another market by extending discounts and manufacturing products under private labels. The CCI in its prima facie order under section 26(1) of the Competition Act, observed that Flipkart operates as a marketplace-based e-commerce platform, which facilitates trade between consumers and third-party sellers. Online platforms offer convenience for third-party sellers as well as consumers. The online marketplace provides a platform for third-party sellers to interact with a larger number of consumers, and it also benefits consumers by saving them time and commuting charges. Hence, CCI determined the relevant product market as services provided by online marketplace platforms for selling goods.

Similarly, the CCI in Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Pvt. Ltd., where it received information from a union of Micro, Small and Mini Enterprises engaged in the trade of mobile phones and other related accessories called Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh, against two big e-commerce platforms, Flipkart and Amazon, which are alleged to be engaged in vertical agreements under section 3(4) of the Competition Act, under which Flipkart and Amazon are preferring certain sellers. Further, it was also alleged that the e-commerce platforms contravene section 4 of the Competition Act by abusing their dominant position by engaging in predatory pricing and imposing unfair conditions. So, CCI passed a prima facie order, directing the DG to investigate the allegations. In this order, the CCI made a similar observation to the Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Pvt. Ltd, and by saying that the online platform offers access to new markets to third-party sellers and increases consumers’ choice of goods and services. An online platform ecosystem provides more benefits than a traditional brick-and-mortar set-up in terms of fixed costs and reach. Again, in Lifestyle Equities C.V. v. Amazon Seller Services Pvt., the CCI passed a dismissal order under section 26(2) of the Competition Act against the allegation against Amazon and Cloudtail. CCI reiterated the distinguishing characteristics of online platforms from offline platforms. CCI further noted that the digital sector is rapidly changing, and the delineation of the online platform cannot have a static approach. Hence, the relevant market should be delineated by considering the market reality and factual circumstances of each case.

[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 2: Anti-competitive Agreements and Competition Law | Section: a0006 - INDIRECT EVIDENCE: ROLE OF CIRCUMSTANTIAL EVIDENCE]

The bidders concerted to manipulate the bidding process by quoting identical rates even if their production costs were different. Also, they collectively abstained from bidding in certain tenders. (Paragraphs 3.14, 6.2) The CCI said that the parallel behaviour of the bidders can be considered evidence of coordinated behaviour on the part of the three bidders regarding the tender. (Paragraph 6.29) 7. T.R. Chandran v. National Egg Co-ordination Committee (NECC). Case Nos 09 and 36 of 2017. The trade association (i.e., NECC) displayed daily egg prices, zone-wise, on its website and monitored the monthly/annual price of eggs at various egg production and consumption centres. (Paragraph 114) Mandating the farmers to follow the price fixed by the NECC is a violation of section 3(3)(a) of the Competition Act. (Paragraphs 153, 208, 209) 8. In Re: Alleged bid-rigging in E-Tenders invited by the Department of Agriculture, Government of Uttar Pradesh for soil sample testing. Suo Motu Case No. 01 of 2020 Some of the cartel members were family friends, and they submitted cover bids under the influence of friendship. (Paragraph 30) The CCI used this evidence as a plus factor to conclude the presence of bid rigging cartel. (Paragraph 88) 9. In Re: Cartelization in Tender Nos 21 and 28 of 2013 of Pune Municipal Corporation for Solid Waste Processing. Suo Motu Case No. 03 of 2016 A director of a 2nd cartel member is the close friend of 1st cartel member, the sole proprietor of 3rd cartel member and the son of 4th cartel member’s director. (Paragraphs 5, 56) Additionally, 2nd cartel member arranged the relevant documents, prepared demand drafts and authorization certificates, and uploaded the tender documents (common IP address) of all cartel members. (Paragraphs 57-58) 4th cartel member aided the 2nd cartel member in fulfilling the eligibility criteria for participation in the tenders. Even though the 4th cartel member was eligible to bid, it did not participate in the tender process and suppressed its bid. (Paragraph 60) Even though the cartel members were separate legal entities and had bid as competitors, they were controlled by one person and had a commonplace for business. (Paragraphs 56-59) There is a meeting of mind among cartel members to rig the bidding process. Once an agreement on bid rigging is established, then it will be presumed to have an adverse effect. The onus to rebut the presumption is on the cartel members. (Paragraph 63) 10. Mr. Rakesh Khare v. Krishna Engineering Works and Others. Reference Case No. 02 of 2020 The CCI took note of the identical pricing in the bids submitted by the cartel members in Eastern Railway tenders for the eight years 2012-19. (Paragraphs 85, 86) By mutually deciding the prices to be quoted, the cartel members were manipulating the tenders. (Paragraph 95) CCI took note of the quotations and found that despite differences in manufacturing, transportation, packaging, and insurance, the cartel members fixed their quotation at INR 17,800. (Paragraphs 87, 88) The cartel members did not indicate the price break-down of their quotations as stipulated in the ‘Instructions to Tenderers for Manual and E-Tender and General Tender Conditions for Open Tenders’ as issued by the procurement authority. This was done with the sole intention of keeping their bids identical. (Paragraph 93) 11. Food Corporation of India v. Shivalik Agro Poly Products Ltd. and Others. Reference Case No. 07 of 2018. From the depositions of the cartel members, it was clear that they had decided to quote an identical price. (Paragraphs 54, 59) The CCI held that the cartel members indulged in bid rigging. (Paragraph 63) 12. In Re: Alleged bid-rigging in E-Tenders invited by the Department of Agriculture, Government of Uttar Pradesh for soil sample testing. Suo Motu Case No. 01 of 2020 Cartel members submitted bids without prior experience in soil testing work and did not have any soil testing machine. (Paragraphs 10 (h), (j), (l)-(n).) One of the cartel members participated in the bidding process to support the designated bidder from being rejected due to insufficient number of bids. (Paragraph 60) To help the designated bidder win, other cartel members indulged in submitting fake invoices and false certificates to act as cover bidders. (Paragraph 132) 13. In Re: Surendra Prasad (MAHAGENCO’s Tender for Coal Liaison service in Maharashtra). Case No. 61 of 2013. The previous record showed the cartel members worked as sub-contractors for each other in various contracts. (Paragraphs 41, 124) Competitors working as sub-contractors for each other shows the anti-competitive arrangement. (Paragraph 125)

[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 6: Digital Markets and Competition Law | Section: a0006 - CHARACTERISTIC FEATURES OF DIGITAL MARKETS]

It is important to note that even the prevalence of multi-homing in a digital market is not a necessary indication of competition in the market. This is because digital platforms often operate in niche markets and target distinct user groups or serve separate purposes which may not be substitutable for other platforms that appear to offer similar services. For instance, in the Play Store case, the CCI observed that multi-homing by app developers across Apple’s iOS and the App Store on the one hand and Google’s Android OS and the Play Store on the other hand, does not tantamount to substitutability as the two OSs serve a different and distinct set of customers. App developers need to maximize their reach and, thus, would not like to confine their offerings exclusively to one of the ecosystems as it would imply losing a sizable portion of the potential consumers’ revenue who are available on the other platform.

Further, the ability to switch between platforms or substitute one platform with another is not the same as multi-homing. This was clarified by the CCI in the Google Android case, wherein it was observed that (m)ulti-homing by app developers should not be confused with demand-side substitution.

The app developers have their apps on both licensable and non-licensable mobile OS, but the fact that app developers have apps both on Play Store and Apple app store does not tantamount to substitutability since the app developers need to have their app on both app stores to reach the maximum consumer base. Thus, these are complementary markets – this does not tantamount to substitutability.

This is because they cannot substitute one OS for the other since Android and iOS users are two different and distinct sets of customers. App developers offer their apps in both ecosystems so as to not lose out on a sizable portion of the potential consumers’ revenue who are available on the other platform.

In the MMT-Go case also, it was argued that consumers multi-home on different channels of hotel booking. On this point, the CCI observed that even if there are consumers who use both these modes (online as well as offline), they did not appear to be using these channels of booking interchangeably for the same booking, given the characteristically different offering that online and offline modes offer. Hence, ‘multi-homing even by the same class of customers may not necessarily imply substitution in the sense used for the purpose of defining markets. Even if, most customers use multiple methods to book hotels his in itself implies that these methods/channels are complementary and not substitutable even from a customer’s perspective.’

Therefore, the position is that evidence of multi-homing by itself does not mean that CCI will exonerate a firm from dominance. Digital markets and the functioning of platforms in these markets are very complex, such that what may seem to be a perceived alternative may not necessarily amount to demand-side substitution. Further, the key part to analyse here is that the relevant market is defined not from the product perspective (i.e., hotel rooms) but from the service perspective (online versus offline booking service). From the cases discussed above, it can be concluded that even though consumers were multi-homing between online and offline channels of hotel booking in the MMT-Go case and app developers had made their apps available across licensable and non-licensable OS in the Google cases, it was not held to be in the same relevant market. Hence, what has to be seen is whether functional substitutes are available and if they are actually substitutable from the perspective of the consumers (both end consumers and B2B).

[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 1: Introduction to Competition Law | Section: a0004 - PROCEDURE FOR INQUIRY]

The Supreme Court of India has succinctly described the ambit of the direction passed under section 26(1) of the Competition Act in the case of SAIL. The Supreme Court noted that the direction of the CCI under section 26(1) of the Competition Act, for the formation of a prima facie opinion requesting the offices of the DG to investigate the matter, is a direction simpliciter to cause an investigation into the matter. The Supreme Court noted that issuance of such a direction is an administrative direction to one of its own wings departmentally and is made without entering upon any adjudicatory process. It does not effectively determine any right or obligation of the parties to the lis. The Supreme Court further noted that a direction under section 26(1) of the Competition Act does not entail civil consequences for any person. In fact, in all the prima facie orders made by the CCI, it is clearly stated that the CCI makes clear that nothing stated in the prima facie order shall be tantamount to a final expression of opinion on the merit of the case, and the DG shall conduct the investigation without being swayed in any manner whatsoever by the observations made herein.

It must be noted herein that although the prima facie order is a direction simpliciter akin to an administrative function, the Gujarat High Court in Vardayani Offset v. Competition Commission of India and Other quashed a prima facie order against the allegation of bid rigging because the CCI failed the test of legality by not recording reasons while passing the prima facie order. Also, it was impossible to read the prima facie order because it was heavily redacted. So, based on the reasoning of the SAIL case, the Gujarat High Court reiterated that the CCI may not record detailed reasons, but it must record some or minimum reasons to show that a prima facie case exists. Hence, the CCI failed to satisfy the basic requirement of a prima facie order, which expresses the mind of the CCI.

Another issue that was deliberated in detail by the Supreme Court in the SAIL case was whether a notice of hearing had to be given to the informant or the alleged infringer prior to passing an order under section 26(1) of the Competition Act. It was noted by the Supreme Court, based on the statutory scheme of the Competition Act that the direction under section 26(1) of the Competition Act could be issued by the CCI with or without hearing the party against whom the information has been filed. Section 19(1) of the Competition Act does not mandate a requirement of notice to be given to the informant, affected party or any other person at the stage of deciding whether a prima facie case exists or not. Such parties cannot claim the right to notice or hearing as a matter of right, but it is always open to the CCI to call any ‘such person’ to render assistance or produce such records as the CCI may consider appropriate.

[Container: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition) | Document: Chapter 5: Intellectual Property and Competition Law | Section: a0007 - SCOPE OF THE IPR EXEMPTION]

The CCI did not agree with the contention of the OEMs and analysed the scope of the exemption and made the following key remarks: (i) Scope of the exemption and when can it be availed: The CCI clarified that section 3(5) of the Competition Act allows an IPR holder to impose ‘reasonable’ conditions to protect his rights which have been conferred or may be conferred upon him under the relevant IPR statues in India. Therefore, the ambit of the exemption is clear that the IPR must have been conferred prior to availing of the exemption under section 3(5) of the Competition Act. Based on the documents submitted by the OEMs, the CCI concluded that the OEMs have not been able to provide documents to substantiate their claim over a particular type of IPR under the applicable Indian IPR laws. Further, some of the OEMS had contended that the overseas parent corporation of the OEMs had validly held IPRs, which were thereafter transferred to the OEMs by means of a validly executed technology transfer agreement. The CCI, however, noted that the IPR claimed by the parent of the OEMs is territorial in nature and is vested upon the IPR holder only in a particular jurisdiction. Thus, in the event that the parent corporation of the OEMs held such IPR rights in the territories where the IPR rights were originally granted, the said rights cannot be said to be granted to the OEMs operating in India by entering into a technology transfer agreement, unless the rights have been granted upon the OEMs which is recognized under the relevant IPR statutes (mentioned in section 3(5) of the Competition Act) in India. Based on the observations of the CCI, for exemption of section 3(5) of the Competition Act to be availed of protection ought to have been granted to the IPR holder, or a process ought to have been initiated for grant of such protection under the relevant IPR statutes in India. (ii) Necessary to protect IPRs – The CCI has adopted an extremely strict interpretation and has held that section 3(5) of the Competition Act only allows the IPR holder to impose reasonable conditions necessary for the protection of the IPRs. Although what is necessary and reasonable would necessarily depend on the facts and circumstances of each case, the CCI in this case held that the restriction imposed on the OES by OEMs not to supply the spare parts in the aftermarket is not necessary to protect their rights guaranteed under the IPR statutes since merely selling spare parts which are manufactured end products, does not necessarily compromise the IPR held by car manufacturers in such products. Further, the CCI observed that the car manufacturers could contractually protect their IPR against these dealers and could still permit them to sell their products.
